issues.description: The guest listing endpoint (PATCH /erpHrm/member/guests) is described as 'an administrative operation for monitoring guest activity' that 'should be restricted to users with appropriate administrative privileges', but no permission check beyond basic member authentication is enforced. Any authenticated member can enumerate all guest records including device fingerprints.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check (e.g., 'admin' or 'org:manage' role) in the provider implementation before returning guest data. Consider whether this endpoint should be restricted to admin-level users only.

issues.description: The organization dashboard endpoint requires 'report:view' permission per its specification, but the controller only enforces basic MemberAuth. The permission check is delegated entirely to the provider with no guard at the controller level, making it easy to miss or bypass.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the provider strictly enforces the 'report:view' permission check and returns 403 if the member lacks it. Consider adding a dedicated permission guard at the controller level for clarity.

issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but does not provide structured logging, log levels, or correlation IDs for production monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Consider replacing console.log with a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston) that supports log levels, correlation IDs, and integration with centralized logging systems.

issues.description: The member search endpoint (PATCH /erpHrm/member/members) allows any authenticated member to search all global member accounts by email and display name. The specification notes 'Organization context does not limit this search (members are global)'. This could enable user enumeration across the entire platform.
issues.suggestion: Consider whether cross-organization member discovery is intentional. If not, scope results to the current organization's members. At minimum, ensure the password field is explicitly excluded in all query paths.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to any of these endpoints. High-frequency operations like timelog creation or search could be abused.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the infrastructure level (e.g., API gateway, NestJS ThrottlerModule) for all endpoints, especially write operations.

issues.description: The password reset endpoint requires `@MemberAuth()` authentication, but password reset is a pre-authentication flow for users who have forgotten their password. Requiring authentication to initiate a password reset defeats the purpose of the feature. Additionally, the `member` payload is passed to the provider but the spec says this endpoint should be accessible to unauthenticated users.
issues.suggestion: Remove the `@MemberAuth()` decorator and `member: MemberPayload` parameter from this endpoint. The password reset request should only require the email address in the body, with no authentication required.

issues.description: The session listing provider accepts arbitrary `erp_hrm_member_id` and `erp_hrm_organization_id` filter parameters from the request body without verifying that the requesting member is authorized to view sessions belonging to other members. This allows any authenticated member to enumerate sessions of other users by supplying a different `erp_hrm_member_id`.
issues.suggestion: Unless the caller has an admin/org:manage permission, enforce that `erp_hrm_member_id` in the where clause is always set to `props.member.id`. Add an authorization check before allowing cross-member session queries.

issues.description: The guest sessions listing provider performs no authorization check whatsoever. Any authenticated member can query all guest sessions across the entire system, including IP addresses, referrers, and browsing history. There is no organization scoping or permission check.
issues.suggestion: Add an authorization check requiring `org:manage` permission and scope the query to the member's current organization context. Verify the member has appropriate administrative privileges before exposing guest session data.

issues.description: The role update authorization check only verifies that the requesting member has an employee record in the role's organization, but does NOT check for `org:manage` permission. Any employee in the organization can update roles, including escalating their own permissions by modifying their assigned role.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check requiring `org:manage` permission before allowing role updates. Also prevent users from modifying the role they are currently assigned to (privilege escalation via self-role modification).

issues.description: The employee lookup for authorization does not scope by organization context from the session. A member could potentially match an employee record from a different organization if they have multiple employee records, bypassing organization isolation.
issues.suggestion: Retrieve the organization context from the member's session first, then scope the employee lookup with `erp_hrm_organization_id: session.erp_hrm_organization_id` to ensure proper multi-tenant isolation.

issues.description: The registration endpoint checks for duplicate email using `findFirst` without a case-insensitive comparison. An attacker could register `User@Example.com` when `user@example.com` already exists, potentially creating duplicate accounts or bypassing email uniqueness constraints depending on database collation.
issues.suggestion: Use case-insensitive email comparison: `where: { email: { equals: props.body.email, mode: 'insensitive' } }` or normalize the email to lowercase before storage and comparison.

issues.description: Both access and refresh tokens are signed with the same `JWT_SECRET_KEY`. If the refresh token is compromised, it cannot be distinguished from an access token by key alone. Additionally, the entire registration flow (member, organization, role, employee, session creation) is not wrapped in a database transaction, leaving the system in an inconsistent state if any step fails.
issues.suggestion: Use separate secrets for access and refresh tokens (e.g., `JWT_ACCESS_SECRET` and `JWT_REFRESH_SECRET`). Wrap the entire registration flow in a `prisma.$transaction()` to ensure atomicity.

issues.description: The active contract check and the new contract creation are not wrapped in a database transaction. A race condition exists where two concurrent requests could both find no active contract and create two contracts simultaneously, violating the business rule of one active contract per employee.
issues.suggestion: Wrap the active contract check, the end-date update of the previous contract, and the new contract creation in a single `prisma.$transaction()` with appropriate isolation level to prevent race conditions.

issues.description: All controller methods use `console.log(error)` for error handling, which may expose sensitive stack traces and internal details to logs without structured context. This is the standard AutoBE pattern.
issues.suggestion: Consider adopting a structured logging library (e.g., Winston, Pino) with log levels and contextual metadata (request ID, user ID) for production observability.

issues.description: The `includeDeleted` flag allows any authenticated member to query soft-deleted organizations. While scoped to organizations the member belongs to, exposing deleted organization data may not be intended for all members.
issues.suggestion: Restrict the `includeDeleted` flag to members with `org:manage` permission, or remove it from the general listing endpoint.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to session listing. Consider adding rate limiting at the infrastructure level.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting via a NestJS throttler guard or API gateway to prevent abuse of session enumeration endpoints.

issues.description: Timer ownership is checked after fetching the timer, but the timer lookup uses findUniqueOrThrow without scoping to the authenticated member. A race condition exists where the timer's deleted_at check happens before the employee ownership check, potentially leaking timer existence information to unauthorized users (timing oracle).
issues.suggestion: Reorder checks: first find the employee record for the member, then query the timer scoped to that employee_id in a single query to avoid information leakage about timer existence.

issues.description: No structured audit logging for timelog access, especially when privileged users (time:view_all) access other employees' timelogs. Sensitive HR data access should be logged.
issues.suggestion: Add audit log entries when privileged users access other employees' time records, including who accessed what and when.

issues.description: Pagination limit is taken directly from user input (props.body.limit) without a maximum cap. A user could request an extremely large number of records (e.g., limit=1000000), causing excessive database load.
issues.suggestion: Enforce a maximum limit, e.g., const limit = Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 20, 100) to prevent resource exhaustion.

issues.description: Duration calculation uses server time (new Date()) which could result in negative or zero durations if the timer was just started. No minimum duration validation is performed before creating the timelog.
issues.suggestion: Add a minimum duration check (e.g., if duration < 0, throw an error) to prevent invalid timelog entries.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/128 files] [3 chunks, 20→20 issues] The codebase follows AutoBE patterns with MemberAuth applied consistently across all endpoints. The main concerns are: (1) the guest listing endpoint exposes device fingerprint data to any authenticated member despite being described as an admin-only operation; (2) the organization dashboard relies solely on provider-level permission enforcement for sensitive aggregate data; (3) the global member search enables cross-organization user enumeration. No injection, cryptographic, or SSRF vulnerabilities are evident in the controller layer. Standard AutoBE patterns (console.log, no rate limiting) are present but treated as suggestions per guidelines.

issues.description: Authorization logic is flawed. It checks if the member is the owner of the timelog's employee, but if not, it checks for a 'time:manage' permission. However, the permission check uses `memberEmployee.erp_hrm_role_id` which is the role of the member's employee in the timelog's organization. This assumes the member has an employee record in that organization, which may not be true. The error handling is also inconsistent: if `memberEmployee` is null, it throws 'Forbidden', but the condition `if (!isOwner) { if (!memberEmployee) { ... } }` could lead to accessing `memberEmployee.erp_hrm_role_id` when `memberEmployee` is null.
issues.suggestion: Restructure authorization: first check if member has an employee record in the organization, then check ownership or permission. Ensure safe access to `memberEmployee` properties.
